name: Red Lionfish
summary: Red lionfish are striped reef fish with long fan-like fins and venomous spines. They are native to the Indo-Pacific and are also famous for becoming invasive in the western Atlantic and Caribbean.
metaTitle: Lionfish Facts | Venomous Spines, Diet & Photos
metaDescription: Discover red lionfish facts with photos, reef habitat, venomous spines, hunting behavior, and invasive range.
speed: Slow swimmer with very fast feeding strikes
adaptations: wide pectoral fins for cornering prey

reproduction.gestationOrIncubation: Eggs hatch in about 1 to 2 days
reproduction.offspringCount: Thousands of eggs in floating egg masses

funFacts: Lionfish use their broad fins to herd prey into tight spaces before striking.
Their spines are venomous, but the fish do not attack people on purpose.
A female lionfish can spawn many times in a year.
Lionfish can swallow prey surprisingly large for their body size.
In the Atlantic, lionfish have spread quickly because they have few natural enemies there.
The red-and-white stripes warn other animals to stay away.

faq.question: What is a lionfish?
faq.answer: A lionfish is a reef fish with long fins, bold stripes, and venomous spines. This page focuses on the red lionfish.

faq.question: Where do lionfish live?
faq.answer: Red lionfish are native to the Indo-Pacific, but they also live as an invasive species in the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, and western Atlantic.

faq.question: What do lionfish eat?
faq.answer: They are carnivores that eat small fish, shrimp, crabs, and other young reef animals.

faq.question: Are lionfish venomous?
faq.answer: Yes. Their long spines carry venom that can cause a very painful sting.

faq.question: Why are lionfish considered invasive?
faq.answer: In the Atlantic they reproduce fast, eat many native reef fish, and face fewer predators than they do in their natural Indo-Pacific range.

faq.question: Are lionfish endangered?
faq.answer: No. Red lionfish are generally listed as Least Concern, but in some places the bigger problem is that they threaten other reef animals.
